events.headline: China announces export licensing on gallium and germanium
events.excerpt: China's Ministry of Commerce announced that exporters of gallium and germanium products would need licenses from 1 August 2023, citing national security. Wrought-metal exports fell sharply the following month. (Reporting: USITC briefing; Reuters; Fastmarkets.)

events.headline: China extends export licensing to graphite products
events.excerpt: China widened its licensing regime to cover certain graphite items, the dominant battery-anode material — applying the gallium/germanium playbook to a core battery input. (Reporting: Fastmarkets; China-Briefing.)

events.headline: China restricts antimony exports — a precision precedent
events.excerpt: China placed export limits on antimony, a related minor metal. Over the following months antimony shipments to the US fell roughly 97% and prices about doubled — a demonstration of how finely licensing can be targeted. (Reporting: Exiger; Andersen Institute.)

events.headline: China bans gallium and germanium exports to the United States
events.excerpt: Licensing escalated to an outright ban on gallium, germanium and antimony exports specifically to the US, including processing technology. By May 2025 gallium traded well above pre-control levels. (Reporting: Fastmarkets; Skillings; Quest Metals.)

events.headline: Lithium futures swing on CATL mine restart signal
events.excerpt: News that CATL was preparing to restart its suspended Jianxiawo lithium mine drove an ~11% correction in Chinese lithium futures — a move analysts estimated exceeded the fundamental supply change several-fold, illustrating speculative amplification. (Reporting: Discovery Alert; Fastmarkets.)

events.headline: Producers curtail lithium and nickel output through 2024
events.excerpt: After lithium fell from ~$71k/t toward ~$10k/t, Australian producers Pilbara Minerals and Mineral Resources curtailed output and mothballed assets; nickel operations were idled as low-cost Indonesian supply pressured prices. (Reporting: Reuters; Fastmarkets; MINING.com.)

events.headline: China places seven heavy rare earths under export control
events.excerpt: China placed seven medium and heavy rare earths — including dysprosium and terbium — and related magnets under export control, applied to all countries. (Reporting: IEA; CSIS; RFF.)

events.headline: CATL's Jianxiawo lithium mine suspended on permit expiry
events.excerpt: CATL's Jianxiawo mine — on the order of 3–6% of global lithium supply — halted after its license expired. Lithium prices jumped and lithium equities rallied worldwide on expectations of broader production discipline. (Reporting: Bloomberg; Axios; Carbon Credits.)

events.headline: US–Australia framework targets rare-earth stockpiles and finance
events.excerpt: A US–Australia framework committed to heavy-rare-earth investment via strategic stockpiles, price support and a $1bn financing pledge, followed by an MoU with Malaysia. (Reporting: Global Policy Watch; CSIS.)

events.headline: China suspends rare-earth and gallium/germanium controls for one year
events.excerpt: Following a US–China trade agreement, China suspended the October 2025 rare-earth controls and the US-specific gallium/germanium ban for roughly one year. The legal framework remains intact and could be reinstated. (Reporting: China-Briefing; Fastmarkets; Clark Hill.)

events.headline: South African PGM producers announce restructuring and shaft closures
events.excerpt: Facing weak palladium and rhodium prices, major South African PGM producers announced restructuring, shaft closures and capital deferrals — output discipline in a market pressured by EV substitution. (Reporting: industry coverage; company statements.)

events.headline: Central-bank gold buying and ETF inflows extend the precious-metals bid
events.excerpt: Surveys indicated the large majority of central banks expected to keep adding gold reserves, and ETF inflows continued — investment and reserve demand, more than mine supply, driving precious-metals prices. (Reporting: BlackRock; Goldman Sachs survey; Sprott.)
